findings.summary: Most data structures reduce to directed graphs + traversal rules. Arrays, trees, linked lists, state machines, neural nets are all special cases.
Problem difficulty is often a function of representation, not inherent complexity. Math problem solving and genetic algorithm effectiveness both confirm this.
Graph isomorphism is not known to be in P, but approximate similarity (graph kernels, WL algorithm) may suffice for pattern recognition.
Hybrid representation (graph structure + primitive attributes at leaves) addresses bloat concern without requiring purely graph-based encoding.
Rule space bloat is the lesser evil compared to data space bloat — rules are static one-time design cost, data is dynamic runtime cost.
Library entries must be rule-invariant pairs, not subgraphs alone. A subgraph only has diagnostic value relative to the rules that produced it during traversal.
Frequency of occurrence alone does not earn library entry. Entries persist only when occurrence correlates with labels.
